Hiking Day 1 — Trail Directions

Thursday, May 21, 2026  •  Kumano Kodo — Nakahechi Route  •  Takijiri-oji to Takahara
Nakahechi Route — Takijiri-oji → Takahara Kiri no Sato
Trail Walk
Step-by-Step Directions Arrives at 17:00 shuttle pickup point — Takahara Kiri no Sato rest area
⌛ Arrival Target: 17:00 at Takahara Kiri no Sato (shuttle pickup) The hike takes 2–3 hours. Start hiking from Takijiri-oji no later than 14:00 to arrive comfortably. The 11:35 AM Ryujin Bus arrives at Takijiri at 12:16 PM — giving a comfortable buffer.
🚌 11:35 AM Ryujin Bus → Takijiri 12:16 PM 🕐 Expected start ~13:00 → Arrive ~15:00–16:00 🏃 No later than 14:00 to depart Takijiri ✅ Arrive by 17:00
3.7–4 km Distance
2–3 hrs Est. Time
+430m / −200m Elevation
2.5 / 5 Difficulty
⚔ Trail Navigation — Staying on the Main Path The trail is bilingual (English & Japanese) and well-signed throughout. Navigation is not the challenge on this section — the steep terrain is. No known problem junctions exist between Takijiri and Takahara.
  • Brown wooden signposts with white lettering mark every junction and destination. A sign at the Takijiri trailhead shows the distance to Takahara (3.7 km).
  • Distance posts every 500 m mark your progress and include emergency contact numbers (police & fire) on each post.
  • Historical markers — oji shrine stones, Jizō statues, and UNESCO World Heritage boundary stones — confirm you are on the correct path throughout.
  • If you encounter an unmarked dirt track heading off into the forest, stop and return to the last signpost before continuing.
⚠ Terrain Note Steep initial ascent. Exposed tree roots and loose rocks throughout. Trail is slippery when wet — take extra care after rain. Hiking poles strongly recommended.
■ Stamp = Official Kumano Kodo stamp station at this waypoint
  1. 1
    Takijiri-oji  滝尻王子  ■ Stamp
    Start of the Nakahechi Route. Register at the Takijiri Pilgrimage Center (Kumano Kodo Kan) inside the building at the trailhead. Purchase your stamp book (¥100) and collect your first stamp. The trail begins directly behind the shrine — look for the stone torii gate.
    🚽 Restroom available here — Last toilet before Takahara (~4 km). Use before starting the trail.
    🌳 Pick up walking sticks and a bear bell at the Kumano Kodo Kan Pilgrimage Center before starting the trail. Both are available at the center and strongly recommended for the steep forested ascent.
    Wikimedia Commons — KMR, CC BY-SA 3.0
  2. 2
    Tainai-kuguri  胎内くぐり
    ~15 min from the start. A narrow passage through a natural cave opening in the rock face. This is a symbolic act of spiritual rebirth central to Kumano pilgrimage tradition — pilgrims have passed through here for centuries. This passage is optional — you can bypass it and continue on the main trail without entering the cave. If you choose to participate: squeeze through the opening to continue upward on the same path. The ascent steepens significantly above this point regardless.
    Photo: Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau
  3. 3
    Chichi-iwa  乳岩 — Milk Rock
    A sacred rock formation whose name comes from legend, not its shape — a Fujiwara couple left their newborn here in the cave during pilgrimage, and a wolf nourished the infant with milk dripping from the rock. The site is associated with prayers for safe childbirth. A natural rest point on the ascent — the trail continues climbing through cedar and cypress forest before easing toward the ridge line.
    Photo: Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau
  4. 4
    Nezu-oji  根来王子
    A historic oji shrine encountered after the steep initial ascent, where the terrain begins to level toward the ridge. One of the named oji stations along this section. A good rest point before the approach to Tsuruginoyama — take a moment before the final climb to the ridgeline.
    Photo: Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau
  5. 5
    Tsuruginoyama Kyozuka  剣ノ山経塚
    A burial mound and religious site at the ridgeline, marking the high point of the day’s trail. Named after the sword-peak (剣ノ山), this is where the steep climb finally levels off before the descent toward Takahara. The views open up considerably from here.
  6. 6
    Takahara Kumano-jinja  高原熊野神社  ■ Stamp
    Shrine at Takahara featuring ancient camphor trees believed to be over 800–1,000 years old. Collect your second stamp here — the stamp box is inside a small hut on the shrine grounds. The village of Takahara opens up around you with sweeping panoramic views of the Hiki River valley below — one of the most photographed spots on the Nakahechi. Take your time before the final short stretch.
    Photo: Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau
  7. 7
    Takahara Kiri no Sato Rest Area — End of Trail
    End of today’s trail section and the 17:00 shuttle pickup point for Guest House Kiyohime. The owner will contact via MyChat / WhatsApp to confirm. Fresh local citrus juice is often available here alongside the panoramic valley view — a well-earned finish. Arrive with time to rest before the shuttle departs.
    🚽 Restroom available here — First toilet since Takijiri. Also has a small shop and public telephone.
    Photo: Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau
Japan Itinerary — May 2026  |  Kumano Travel Res #186433
